sanskrit_devanagari:
जातस्य हि ध्रुवो मृत्युर्ध्रुवं जन्म मृतस्य च ।
तस्मादपरिहार्येऽर्थे न त्वं शोचितुमर्हसि ॥ २७ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
jātasya hi dhruvo mṛtyur dhruvaṁ janma mṛtasya ca |
tasmād aparihārye 'rthe na tvaṁ śocitum arhasi || 27 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical; line-break inserted between hemistichs in source pack)
exact (IAST text body identical to vedabase)
exact (academic critical edition; Bhg_02.027 = MBh_06,024.027). Body text identical.

translations.verbatim_quote: One who has taken his birth is sure to die, and after death one is sure to take birth again. Therefore, in the unavoidable discharge of your duty, you should not lament.
Death is certain for one who has been born, and rebirth is inevitable for one who has died. Therefore, you should not lament over the inevitable.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: One has to take birth according to one's activities of life. And after finishing one term of activities, one has to die to take birth for the next. In this way one is going through one cycle of birth and death after another without liberation.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.27 This being so, 'death of anyone born', etc. Hi, for; mrtyuh, death; jatasya, of anyone born; dhruvah, is certain; is without exception; ca, and mrtasya, of the dead; janmah, (re-)birth; is dhruvam, a certainty. Tasmat, therefore, this fact, viz birth and death, is inevitable.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the verse as completing the conditional argument of 2.26: granting the premise that the Self is born and dies with each body, the cycle is structurally inevitable. Death follows birth and birth follows death without exception. Grief over what is structurally inevitable is irrational. The argument is intentionally minimal — it does not depend on metaphysical commitments; it depends only on observation.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.27 For what has originated, destruction is certain — it is seen to be inevitable. Similarly what has perished will inevitably originate. Origination, annihilation etc., are merely particular states of an existent entity.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja sharpens the argument: birth and destruction are not events that happen TO entities — they are states of entities. To be born is to be the kind of thing that perishes; to perish is to be the kind of thing that re-originates. The two are paired by structure, not by accident. Mourning a death is mourning a thing for being the kind of thing it definitionally is.

positions.rendering: in the unavoidable discharge of your duty
this fact (artha = matter / fact), viz birth and death, is inevitable
the unavoidable matter — origination and destruction are paired states

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Prabhupada's rendering inserts 'discharge of your duty' which is interpretive (linking the verse forward to the duty argument that begins at 2.31). The literal Sanskrit is 'aparihārye arthe' — 'in the unavoidable matter/fact.' Shankara reads artha as 'fact,' Ramanuja as 'matter/state.' Mukundananda's 'over the inevitable' is closer to the literal. The disagreement is over whether the verse already pivots to duty (Prabhupada) or stays on the metaphysical inevitability of the birth-death cycle (Shankara, Ramanuja). The traditional commentaries read it as still on the metaphysical leg of the argument; the duty pivot proper begins at 2.31.

literal_meaning: For one who is born, death is certain; and for one who has died, birth is certain. Therefore, in this unavoidable matter, you should not grieve.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The conclusion of the conditional argument begun in 2.26. Granting the materialist premise that the Self is born and dies along with the body, the cycle of birth and death is structurally inevitable. Grief over what is structurally inevitable is incoherent — it is grief over a thing for being the kind of thing it is. The verse is the secular complement of the imperishable doctrine: even if you reject the imperishable, the conclusion that grief is misplaced still holds. Both branches converge on 'do not grieve.' The commentarial tradition treats 2.26-2.27 as a single argumentative unit closing the materialist escape route.
